hard_gates.description: Entire career at pure consulting/services firms, no product co
AI keyword stuffer on a non-technical career (framework trap)

weighted_requirements.description: Production embeddings-based retrieval deployed to users
Vector DB / hybrid search operational experience

latent_requirements: Ability to thrive in a rapidly changing, early-stage (Series A) startup environment, where the role's scope evolves frequently.
Comfort with ambiguity and a willingness to define problems and solutions from scratch.
Strong bias towards shipping functional, even if imperfect, solutions quickly to gather user feedback.
Deep technical expertise in modern ML systems (embeddings, retrieval, ranking, LLMs, fine-tuning) combined with a scrappy product-engineering mindset.
Ownership of the entire 'intelligence layer' of the product, from architecture to evaluation.
Proactive identification and prioritization of high-leverage technical improvements.
Proficiency in designing and implementing robust evaluation frameworks (offline benchmarks, A/B testing, feedback loops).
Experience beyond basic LLM API usage, demonstrating understanding of pre-LLM era retrieval and ranking fundamentals.
Ability to mentor and grow a new team.
Excellent written communication skills, accustomed to async-first work.
Comfort with open debate and rapid decision-making.
Willingness to commit to the company for 3+ years.
Proven ability to ship end-to-end ranking, search, or recommendation systems at scale in a product company setting.
Strong, defensible opinions on ML system design and evaluation, backed by practical experience.
Active engagement in the job market or demonstrable availability for new roles.
Practical, production-focused experience with ML, not solely academic or research-oriented.
Strong Python skills and attention to code quality.
Willingness to relocate to Pune/Noida or be from Tier-1 Indian cities, with a flexible hybrid cadence and quarterly travel.
Preferably a notice period under 30 days.
anti_patterns: Seeking a well-scoped role with a defined career ladder (characteristic of large tech companies like Google/Meta).
Desire to 'just code' without engaging with product strategy, recruiter workflows, or evaluation frameworks.
Career primarily in pure research environments without production deployment experience.
AI experience limited to recent (under 12 months) LangChain/OpenAI API projects without foundational ML production experience.
Senior engineers who haven't actively written production code in the last 18 months due to architecture/tech lead roles.
Career trajectory focused on optimizing for titles ('title-chasers') by frequently switching companies (every ~1.5 years).
Individuals whose primary focus is on new frameworks rather than underlying system design ('framework enthusiasts').
Exclusive career experience at consulting firms (TCS, Infosys, Wipro, Accenture, Cognizant, Capgemini) without prior product company exposure.
Primary expertise in computer vision, speech, or robotics without significant NLP/IR background.
Lack of external validation (papers, talks, open-source contributions) for work done entirely on closed-source proprietary systems for 5+ years.
Need for a stable, mature codebase to be productive.
Discomfort with written communication, open disagreement, or fast-paced, sometimes unstable environments.
Candidates who are passively looking or disengaged (e.g., inactive on platform, low response rates).
boilerplate_removed: Rejection of the idea of a fixed 'checklist' JD that doesn't evolve.
Explicitly stating that the role definition will change every six months.
Redefining 'experience required' as a judgment range rather than a strict number of years.
Avoiding the typical JD practice of listing 20 skills and expecting candidates to have all.
Including a dedicated 'Things we explicitly do NOT want' section, which is often omitted in JDs.
Warning against superficial keyword matching (e.g., 'AI keywords') as a substitute for contextual understanding of a candidate's experience.
Explicitly stating that a perfect skills list does not guarantee fit if the background is irrelevant (e.g., Marketing Manager with AI keywords).
Emphasizing that behavioral signals (e.g., activity, response rate) are crucial and can down-weight otherwise perfect-on-paper candidates.
Directly addressing the gap between what a JD 'says' and what it 'means'.
